_meta.notes: Chapter 1 Lesson 10. 4 words instead of 3 (tayo/kami are a paired concept). Previously taught: kumusta, mabuti, ikaw, magandang, umaga, gabi, po, naman, sige, oo, hindi, opo, salamat, walang anuman, pasensya na, siyempre, pare, mare, ako, ka, siya.

cards.body: Tagalog makes a distinction that English doesn't. There are two words for 'we,' and using the wrong one changes the meaning.
Tayo means 'we' including the person you're talking to.

example.english: How are we doing? (you and me, together)
example.note: Use tayo when the listener is part of the group.

cards.body: Kami also means 'we,' but it excludes the person you're talking to.

example.context: Telling a friend about your weekend with other friends
example.english: We're doing good. (me and others, not you)
example.note: Use kami when the listener is NOT part of the group.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: This inclusive/exclusive 'we' distinction exists in many Asian and Pacific languages but not in European ones. English speakers often find it the trickiest Tagalog concept.

cards.source_text: You're inviting your friend Jun to eat. You want Jun to come with you.
cards.explanation: Tayo, because you're including Jun. Kami would mean 'we, but not you.'

cards.body: Kayo means 'you all.' It's also the formal/respectful 'you' for one person.

example.plural: Kumusta kayo? = How are you all?
example.formal: Kumusta po kayo? = How are you? (respectful, to one elder)
example.note: Like 'vous' in French. Plural or formal singular.

cards.prompt: Which is the most respectful way to ask how she is?
cards.explanation: Kumusta po kayo? uses both po (respect) and kayo (formal you). The most respectful greeting.

cards.body: Sila means 'they' or 'them.' The plural of siya.

example.note: Siya = one person. Sila = multiple people.

cards.source_text: You went on a trip with friends. Your coworker (who didn't go) asks how it was.
cards.explanation: Kami, because your coworker wasn't on the trip. Tayo would include them.

cards.prompt: Ask a group of friends: How are you all?
cards.explanation: Kumusta kayo? = How are you all?

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue. User must get ALL questions right to complete. Cannot finish lesson with outstanding wrong answers.
wrong_answer_tracking.wrong_count_usage: Feed to Gemini system prompt for AI Practice.

alam_mo_ba: The tayo/kami distinction prevents a real ambiguity that English has. 'We went to the beach' in English leaves you guessing whether the listener was there.
Kayo as a formal 'you' for one person works just like 'vous' in French or 'usted' in Spanish.
You now know all the Tagalog pronouns: ako, ka/ikaw, siya, tayo, kami, kayo, sila. That's the full set.
